scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim describes the internal ethical doctrines regarding 'the sage' in the context of Diogenes Laertius's compilation of Greek philosophical schools.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule summarizes a general Hellenistic philosophical ideal (c. -300 to 0); -150 serves as a representative midpoint for these developed dogmas.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The event involves Phaedo and Socrates in Athens, where the status of 'free man' was a prerequisite for certain civic and intellectual pursuits.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Phaedo was part of Socrates' circle in the final years of Socrates' life (d. -399).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The interaction occurs between Pericles, a leading Athenian statesman, and Simon regarding the Athenian civic value of parrhesia (free speech).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Pericles was at the height of his influence in Athens during the mid-5th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text references the Areopagus (Athenian high court) and Demetrius of Phalerum (an Athenian statesman/philosopher).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Demetrius of Phalerum governed Athens as a Macedonian-backed regent starting in approximately -317.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The choregia was a specific Athenian liturgy (public service) funded by wealthy individuals like Dion for citizens like Plato.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This refers to the period during Plato's middle and late career when he was associated with Dion of Syracuse.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim lists specific battles (Tanagra, Corinth, Delium) involving Athenian citizen-soldiers like Socrates (often confused with Plato in later anecdotes).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Battle of Delium took place in -424; it was a major engagement for the Athenian citizen army during the Peloponnesian War.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the Athenian system of cleruchies (citizen settlements) on the island of Aegina.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Athenians expelled the Aeginetans and established a cleruchy at the start of the Peloponnesian War in -431.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule refers to a specific Aeginetan law targeting Athenian citizens landing on their island.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The anecdote refers to the intense early 5th-century enmity between Athens and Aegina before the Persian Wars.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The assembly mentioned is that of the Aeginetans deciding the fate of a captured enemy (Plato, according to the tradition).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Tradition dates Plato's capture and sale into slavery by the Aeginetans to roughly -388.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule refers to general Greek religious norms regarding Dionysian festivals which were observed across many poleis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule is attributed to Plato; -348 is the year of his death, representing his floruit.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The interaction involves Plato and Xenocrates, two philosophers active in the Athenian Academy, discussing household management.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule reflects norms of slave punishment during Plato's lifetime (d. -348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim is from Plato's will, a legal document governing property in the Athenian deme of Iphistiadae.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The provisions of the will were enacted upon Plato's death in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim lists slaves and manumission in the context of an Athenian philosopher's will.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This reflects the legal status of slaves and manumission procedures at the time of Plato's death.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This reflects a general Aristotlean or Peripatetic doctrine regarding natural hierarchy, recorded by Diogenes Laertius.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This doctrine is characteristic of the 4th-century BCE Peripatetic school; -322 is the death of Aristotle.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim specifically identifies the kingship of the Lacedaemonians and Macedonians as hereditary.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This general description of Greek political systems fits the mid-4th century BCE when both Sparta and Macedon were dominant powers.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The definition describes oligarchy, a standard form of government in many Greek poleis like Corinth or Megara.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This political theory was standardized in the mid-4th century BCE by thinkers like Aristotle.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim explicitly names Carthage and describes its legal system regarding the acquisition of office.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This observation about Carthage was famously shared by Aristotle in his Politics (written mid-4th century BCE).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes unwritten laws (agraphos nomos) prevalent in the social life of Greek city-states.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This reflects a general Hellenistic normative ethics common in the period spanning the midpoint of the work's chronological range.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule concerns the management of the Academy in Athens after the death of Plato.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Speusippus took over the Academy immediately after Plato's death in -348/7.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim describes a specific exception granted by the Athenian citizen assembly to a resident philosopher.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Xenocrates was head of the Academy from -339 to -314, the period when his public reputation in Athens was highest.
